# Real-Time Collaborative Notepad with Qwik City + Server-Sent Events

## Background

You are building a shared collaborative notepad using the **Qwik** framework and **Qwik City** meta-framework. Multiple browser tabs can edit the same document, and edits made in one tab appear in every other connected tab in real time. Real-time delivery is implemented with **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** served directly from a Qwik City endpoint, and edits are broadcast to all connected clients through an **in-memory publish/subscribe** hub that also remembers the latest document text.

Everything runs locally. There are **no external services, databases, or third-party APIs** — the pub/sub hub, the SSE stream, and the update endpoint all live inside the Qwik City app.

A base Qwik City project has already been scaffolded and its dependencies installed at the project path below. Your job is to implement the notepad feature.

## Requirements

* Implement a single Qwik City **endpoint** file that serves two HTTP methods on the same route `/api/notepad`:
  * `GET`: opens a long-lived **SSE** stream (`Content-Type: text/event-stream`). Immediately upon connection it sends the current document text as a `snapshot` event, then forwards every subsequent edit as an `update` event.
  * `POST`: accepts a JSON edit, updates the server-held document text, broadcasts the edit to all currently connected SSE clients, and returns a small JSON acknowledgement.
* Implement an **in-memory pub/sub hub** that keeps the latest document text and a set of active subscribers. Broadcasting an edit must reach every connected SSE client. Connections must be cleaned up (unsubscribed) when a client disconnects.
* Implement the notepad UI at the index route `/` as a Qwik `component$`:
  * Render a `<textarea>` bound to reactive state (`useSignal` or `useStore`).
  * Open the SSE connection from inside `useVisibleTask$` using the browser `EventSource` API, reconcile incoming `snapshot` and `update` events into the reactive state, and close the connection in the task `cleanup`.
  * When the user types, send the new text to the `POST` endpoint. An edit originating from a given client must not clobber that same client's in-progress text when it is echoed back (ignore your own broadcasts by comparing a client id).

## Implementation Hints

* Project path: /home/user/qwik-app
* Start command: `npm run dev -- --port 3000 --host 0.0.0.0`
* Port: 3000
* Use a Qwik City `index.ts` endpoint with `onGet` / `onPost` request handlers. For SSE, write to the response using `requestEvent.getWritableStream()` and a `TextEncoder`; set the response headers to `text/event-stream`. Keep server-only pub/sub logic out of client bundles (keep it in a module imported only by the endpoint).
* Remember Qwik's serialization and hook rules: all `use*` hooks must be called synchronously at the top level of the component, and browser-only APIs like `EventSource` belong inside `useVisibleTask$`.
* **SSE wire format (must match exactly so the stream is machine-readable):**
  * The initial event MUST be:
    ```
    event: snapshot
    data: {"text":"<current document text>"}

    ```
  * Each broadcast edit MUST be sent as:
    ```
    event: update
    data: {"text":"<new text>","clientId":"<originating client id>"}

    ```
  * Each event ends with a blank line (`\n\n`). The `data:` line MUST be a single-line JSON object.
* **POST `/api/notepad`** request body is JSON `{"text": string, "clientId": string}`. It MUST update the stored document text, broadcast an `update` event carrying that same `text` and `clientId` to all connected SSE clients, and respond with HTTP 200 and body `{"ok": true}`.
* **GET `/api/notepad`** MUST respond with header `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and MUST emit exactly one `snapshot` event (reflecting the latest stored text) as the first event on every new connection.
* The index route `/` MUST render a `<textarea>` element and MUST establish the `EventSource` connection to `/api/notepad` from within `useVisibleTask$`.
Let

Qwik City setup.

# example for cloudflare worker server-sent events
created: 2025-10-06, last updated: 2025-10-16, [Read more](https://gist.github.com/bouroo/4b91e535a7f036cb8ab2ba4cb16ae88e)

# Server-sent events: a simple way to stream ...
created: 2021-01-12, last updated: 2026-07-15, [Read more](https://jvns.ca/blog/2021/01/12/day-36--server-sent-events-are-cool--and-a-fun-bug/)

I was SO DELIGHTED to learn that they’re just HTTP requests.
...
$ curl -N 'http://localhost:3000/sessions/15/stream'
event: panda
data: one
...
Here’s what the browser Javascript code to stream server-sent events looks like.
(I got this example from the MDN page on server-sent events)
You can either subscribe to all events, or have different handlers for
different types of events.
Here I have a handler that just receives events with
...
```
const evtSource = new EventSource("/sessions/15/stream", { withCredentials: true })
evtSource.addEventListener("panda", function(event) {
console.log("status", event)
});
```
...
def handler
# SSE is Rails' built in server-sent events thing
sse = SSE.new(response.stream, event: "status")
sse.write('event')
sleep 1
sse.write('another event')
end
```
...
# Turn off buffering in nginx
...
10.times do
sse.write('event')
sleep 1
end
end
...
That github issue suggested a workaround I could apply to just the streaming endpoint, which was to set the

# How do server-sent events actually work?
created: 2011-10-03, last updated: 2025-08-29, [Read more](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7636165/how-do-server-sent-events-actually-work)

On the server side, it's rather simple.
All you really need to do is set the following http headers:
```
Content-Type: text/event-stream
Cache-Control: no-cache
Connection: keep-alive
```
Be sure to respond with the code
```
200
```
and not
```
204
```
or any other code, as this will cause compliant user agents to disconnect.
Also, make sure to not end the connection on the server side.
You are now free to start pushing messages down that connection.
In nodejs (using express), this might look something like the following:
```
app.get("/my-stream", function(req, res) {
res.status(200)
.set({ "content-type" : "text/event-stream"
, "cache-control" : "no-cache"
, "connection" : "keep-alive"
})
res.write("data: Hello, world!\n\n")
})
```
On the client, you just use the
```
...
```
...
You also need to make sure to call
```
res.flushHeaders()
```
, otherwise Node.js won't send the HTTP headers until you call
...
res.end()
```

# useVisibleTask$() | Tutorial 📚 Qwik Documentation
created: 2023-06-25, last updated: 2025-12-21, [Read more](https://qwik.dev/tutorial/hooks/use-visible-task/)

Use
`useVisibleTask$()` to execute code after the component is resumed.
This is useful for setting up timers or streams on the client when the application is resumed.
...
`useVisibleTask$()` receives a
`track()` function just like
`useTask$()`.
Use the
`track()` function to trigger the effect when the store is updated.
...
Client effect code is executed after the component is resumed.
The
`useVisibleTask$()` method takes an additional argument (
`{strategy:'intersection-observer|document-ready|document-idle'}`) that controls when the effect is executed.
There are three options:
...
### Example
The example shows a clock component that is rendered below the fold.
Use the
`useVisibleTask$()` to make the clock update the current time every second to make it work on the client.
We have provided the utility function
`updateClock` to aid your implementation.
Keep in mind that
`useVisibleTask$()` should return a cleanup function that releases the
`setInterval` timer so that the timer can be properly cleaned up when the component is unmounted/destroyed.

# Should You Use Tasks in Qwik?
created: 2024-03-10, last updated: 2025-08-06, [Read more](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CXyMnqEgZ3I)

useVisibleTask$() runs only in the browser,
{ts:265} and it is eagerly executed when the component becomes visible.
...
One issue that I want
{ts:455} to point out here is that we have to clean up our event listener on
{ts:460} the window object.
If we forget this, we have a potential memory leak.
{ts:466} So this is how we could implement a scroll by with the useVisibleTask$() hook.
...
{ts:609} In this case we can use useVisibleTask$() again.
A useVisibleTask$() solution can
{ts:616} look like this.
…
{ts:641} So we set the results to an empty array and return.
If the query is not empty, we
{ts:648} set the loading status to true.
Then we fetch the data and assign it to the results and
{ts:658} reset the loading status to false.
Remember that useVisibleTask$()
...
Another important use case of useVisibleTask$() is synchronous
{ts:1130} event handling.

# [🐞] useTask$/useVisibleTask$ produce unexpected behavior · Issue #4210 · QwikDev/qwik
created: 2023-05-13, last updated: 2026-04-17, [Read more](https://github.com/QwikDev/qwik/issues/4210)

2. Start the server (`npm run preview`)
3. Open the site
...
6. See that using `<Link>` to navigate back does end up in the `mounted` state, then `unmounted` after that
7. Uncomment the `useVisibleTask$` in src/routes/index.tsx
8. Restart the server
...
All `cleanup` are called when the application is paused.
Imagine a server does setTimeout(), we need to cleanup timer etc.
it's expected behaviour
...
My understanding of useTask was missing the detail that a useTask's cleanup is only called on component unmount if the task ran a second time on the client.
...
The cleanup is called when:
- the same task runs again
- the component is unmounted
- the container is paused
Contributor
...
The reason is that it's usually necesary to cleanup things when moving between server and client, imagine, a setTimeout will not cross from the server to the client
...
By the way, there isn't always a cleanup to run when a component is unmounted, which is the thing I learned.
This playground shows that - the first time the component is unmounted, since the task never ran on the client, there's no cleanup to do.

- Snapshot and update events directly set text.value
- The

textarea's rendered value follows text.value

This is clean and explicit.

For the
input handler, I want to debounce the POST to avoid spamming the server on every
keystroke. Let me use a simple debouncer inside onInput

$.

Actually, re-reading the requirements:
> When the user types, send the new text to the

POST endpoint.

This doesn't require debouncing. But in practice, sending every
keystroke on fast typing could overload the server. Let me keep it simple and just send every keystroke - that's

what's expected.

Actually wait, looking more carefully:
> An edit originating from a given client must not
clobber that same client's in-progress text when it is echoed back (ignore your own broadcasts

by comparing a client id).

So the test is that I MUST compare clientIds
and ignore my own broadcasts. But I think the simpler interpretation is: I send
the entire text on every keystroke and the server echoes back to all
clients including me. So my own update events come back to me
, and I should ignore them (since I already updated the textarea in

response to my keystroke).

OK so:
- User types "h
" -> signal updated to "h", POST sent with text="h",

clientId="X"
- Server: broadcasts to all clients including

"X"
- My client gets the update event: `

{"text": "h", "clientId": "X"}`
- I see client

Id matches, so I ignore the event and don't update the signal

If

another client "Y" types "i":
- POST sends

with text="i", clientId="Y"
- Server: broadcasts to all clients including "X

"
- My client gets update: `{"text": "i", "clientId": "Y

"}`
- I see clientId != "X", so I update the signal to "i

"

This means the textarea shows the latest text from any client.

OK now
let me think about a potential issue: every keystroke from the user triggers a POST.
If the user types "hello", there will be 5 POST requests. Each one returns
an update event echoed back to me (which I ignore) AND
to all other clients. They will see "h", "he", "hel", "hell",

"hello" in succession. That's fine for a demo.

OK let me also
think about the client ID. I need to generate a unique ID per tab. `Math
.random().toString(36).slice(2)` or `crypto.randomUUID()` should work

. The test won't care about the format, just that it's unique.

Now let me also think

about the JSON encoding. The format spec says:
```
event: update
data:
entire `data:` line should be a single line of valid JSON. When the
text contains special chars like newlines or quotes, we need to use `JSON.stringify` to
properly escape them. JSON.stringify produces a single-line JSON string (no embedded

newlines), so that satisfies "a single-line JSON object".

Wait,

but the spec says:
> The `data:` line MUST be a single

-line JSON object.

So `JSON.stringify({text: "hi", client
Id: "x"})` produces `'{"text":"hi","clientId

":"x"}'` which is a single line. 

But there could be edge cases where the
text contains a `\n` literal. JSON.stringify will properly escape it as `

\\n`, so the output stays on one line. 

For the

snapshot event:
```
event: snapshot
data: {"text":"<current
<text>"}` - I'm just JSON encoding the

text into the value field. No clientId needed.

OK let me now write everything.
First the hub, then the endpoint, then the index page.
Also let me think about: the user might not have experience with
crypto.randomUUID on older browsers. Let

me use a simpler approach:

```typescript
const clientId = `${Date
.now().toString(36)}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(

()` if available, with fallback.

Actually since this
is for a Qwik app running in modern browsers (2024+

), I can use `crypto.randomUUID()`.

Let me write the final implementation now.
One more concern: I should not break Qwik's serialization rules
. The `useSignal` for the EventSource needs to be a No
Serialize wrapper, since EventSource can't be serialized. I'll use `

noSerialize()`.

Actually wait - in Qwik, signals are serialized (
just the value). If I store an EventSource directly in
a signal, Qwik will try to serialize it. The proper pattern is to either

:
1. Use `noSerialize()` to wrap non-serializable values
2. Use `
useStore` with a flag like `isSignal` or just keep the EventSource

in a variable in the closure

Since the `useVisibleTask$` callback is server-r
endered on the client, and the cleanup is called when the component is unmounted, I
don't strictly need to store the EventSource in a signal. I just

need a local variable in the closure.

But for the initial render, Qwik
generates the callback twice - once on server (no-op) and once on
client. I should use `useVisibleTask$` which runs only on the client.
Let me also think about the cleanup: the cleanup function from `useVisibleTask$
` is called when the component unmounts. I should call `es.close()` there.

<Subscriber>;
}

Create the API endpoint at `/home/user/qwik-app/src/routes/api/notepad/index.ts` 3. Update the index route at `/home/user/qwik-app/src/routes/index.tsx` to render the notepad UI Let me think about how the dev server works in Qwik: - The dev mode runs Vite with SSR - For SSE endpoints, I need to write to the response stream and keep it open - I need to set headers including `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and possibly `Cache-Control: no-cache` and `Connection: keep-alive` - I need to ensure the SSE endpoint can be properly subscribed to For the pub/sub hub: - Single in-memory module-level Set of subscriber callbacks (writers) - A text variable that holds the current document text - Functions: getText(), subscribe(fn), unsubscribe(fn), broadcast(text, clientId), update(text) Let me draft the pub/sub hub: ```typescript // /home/user/qwik-app/src/lib/notepad-hub.ts type ClientId = string; interface Subscriber { write: (chunk: string) => Promise; close: () => Promise; } let documentText = ""; const subscribers = new Set(); export function getText(): string { return documentText; } export function updateAndBroadcast(text: string, clientId: ClientId): void { documentText = text; const payload = { event: "update", data: JSON.stringify({ text, clientId }), }; const message = `event: update\ndata: ${JSON.stringify({ text, clientId })}\n\n`; for (const subscriber of subscribers) { subscriber.write(message).catch(() => { // ignore write failures - they'll be cleaned up via abort signal }); } } export function subscribe(subscriber: Subscriber): () => void { subscribers.add(subscriber); return () => { subscribers.delete(subscriber); }; } ``` Wait, I need to think about whether the hub must be a singleton across multiple module instances. In Vite dev mode, modules might be re-loaded multiple times. close: () => Promise; } const g = globalThis as any; if (!g.__qwikNotepadHub) { g.__qwikNotepadHub = { text: "", subscribers: new Set(), }; } const state: NotepadState = g.__qwikNotepadHub; export function getText(): string { return state.text; } export async function broadcastUpdate(text: string, clientId: string): Promise { state.text = text; const message = `event: update\ndata: ${JSON.stringify({ text, clientId })}\n\n`; // Iterate over a copy to avoid issues with concurrent unsubscribe for (const subscriber of Array.from(state.subscribers)) { try { await subscriber.write(message); } catch { // ignore write errors } } } export function subscribe(subscriber: Subscriber): () => void { state.subscribers.add(subscriber); return () => { state.subscribers.delete(subscriber); }; } ``` Now for the endpoint: ```typescript // /home/user/qwik-app/src/routes/api/notepad/index.ts import type { RequestHandler } from "@builder.io/qwik-city"; import { getText, broadcastUpdate, subscribe, } from "~/lib/notepad-hub"; export const onGet: RequestHandler = async (requestEvent) => { const encoder = new TextEncoder(); requestEvent.headers.set("Content-Type", "text/event-stream"); const writableStream = requestEvent.getWritableStream(); const writer = writableStream.getWriter(); let closed = false; const subscriber = { write: async (chunk: string) => { if (closed) return; await writer.write(encoder.encode(chunk)); }, close: async () => { if (closed) return; closed = true; try { await writer.close(); } catch {} }, }; const unsubscribe = subscribe(subscriber); // Handle client disconnect requestEvent.signal.addEventListener("abort", () => { unsubscribe(); subscriber.close().catch(() => {}); }); // Send initial snapshot const snapshot = `event: snapshot\ndata: ${JSON.stringify({ text: getText() })}\n\n`; await writer.write(encoder.encode(snapshot)); }; export const onPost: RequestHandler = async (requestEvent) => { const body = await requestEvent.parseBody() as { text: string; clientId: string } | null; const text = body?.text ?? ""; const clientId = body?.clientId ?? ""; await broadcastUpdate(text, clientId); requestEvent.json(200, { ok: true }); }; ``` Now for the index route: ```typescript // /home/user/qwik-app/src/routes/index.tsx import { $, component$, useSignal, useVisibleTask$, noSerialize, type NoSerialize, } from "@builder.io/qwik"; import type { DocumentHead } from "@builder.io/qwik-city"; export default component$(() => { const text = useSignal(""); const myClientId = useSignal(""); const eventSource = useSignal | undefined>(undefined); useVisibleTask$(({ cleanup }) => { // Generate a client id const clientId = `${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}`; myClientId.value = clientId; const es = new EventSource("/api/notepad"); eventSource.value = noSerialize(es); es.addEventListener("snapshot", (event) => { try { const data = JSON.parse((event as MessageEvent).data); text.value = data.text ?? input.command:
# Start an SSE subscriber in the background and capture its output
( curl -sN http://localhost:3000/api/notepad > /tmp/sse-out.txt ) &
SSE_PID=$!
sleep 0.5
# Now POST an update
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/notepad \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text":"broadcast me","clientId":"client-abc"}' >/dev/null
sleep 0.5
# Kill the subscriber
kill $SSE_PID 2>/dev/null
wait 2>/dev/null
echo "----- SSE OUTPUT -----"
cat /tmp/sse-out.txt

# Start two SSE subscribers in the background
( curl -sN http://localhost:3000/api/notepad > /tmp/sse-out-A.txt ) &
PID_A=$!

wait 2>/dev/null

input.result:
Implemented the real-time collaborative notepad for the Qwik City app at `/home/user/qwik-app`.

**Files created/modified**

1. **`src/lib/notepad-hub.ts`** (new) — server-only in-memory pub/sub hub.
   - Stores the latest document text and a `Set<Subscriber>` of active SSE writers.
   - Singleton state is anchored to `globalThis` so Vite HMR re-evaluations don't drop subscribers or reset the document.
   - Exports `getText()`, `broadcastUpdate(text, clientId)`, and `subscribe({write, close})` that returns an unsubscribe function.

2. **`src/routes/api/notepad/index.ts`** (new) — Qwik City endpoint with two HTTP method handlers on the same route.
   - `onGet`: sets `Content-Type: text/event-stream` (plus `Cache-Control: no-cache, no-transform`, `Connection: keep-alive`, `X-Accel-Buffering: no`), writes the initial `event: snapshot\ndata: {"text":"..."}\n\n`, registers the writer with the hub, and wires `requestEvent.signal`'s `abort` event to unsubscribe + close the stream — so disconnects are cleaned up.
   - `onPost`: parses `{"text", "clientId"}`, calls `broadcastUpdate(...)`, and responds `requestEvent.json(200, { ok: true })`.
   - Exact wire format used for each message:
     ```
     event: snapshot
     data: {"text":"..."}
     
     ```
     ```
     event: update
     data: {"text":"...","clientId":"..."}
     
     ```

3. **`src/routes/index.tsx`** (modified) — Qwik `component$` notepad UI.
   - `<textarea>` bound to a `useSignal("")`.
   - A `useVisibleTask$` generates a per-tab id (via `crypto.randomUUID()` with a fallback), opens `new EventSource("/api/notepad")`, listens for `snapshot` and `update` events, ignores updates whose `clientId` matches the local id (so the client's own echoes never clobber in-progress text), and cleans up the EventSource in the `cleanup` callback.
   - `onInput$` updates the local signal immediately and POSTs `{text, clientId}` to `/api/notepad`.

**Verification performed against the running dev server**

- `GET /api/notepad` → `HTTP/1.1 200`, `content-type: text/event-stream`, first event is exactly `event: snapshot\ndata: {"text":"hello world"}\n\n`.
- `POST /api/notepad` with `{"text":"...","clientId":"..."}` → `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`, `content-type: application/json`, body `{"ok":true}`.
- Two simultaneous SSE subscribers each receive the snapshot followed by every `update` event produced by a POST (`event: update\ndata: {"text":"...","clientId":"..."}\n\n`), confirming pub/sub fan-out.
- `/` renders the `<textarea>` with the proper `on:input` handler and the documented document head.
